question: Explain the concept of Green IT and list any four benefits of implementing Green IT practices in organizations.
expectedPoints: Green IT refers to using technology sustainably to minimize environmental impact
Benefits: 1) Reduced energy consumption and costs 2) Lower carbon footprint 3) E-waste reduction 4) Improved corporate image 5) Regulatory compliance 6) Cost savings on hardware

question: Define PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) and explain how it is used to measure data center efficiency.
expectedPoints: PUE is the ratio of total facility energy to IT equipment energy
Formula: PUE = Total Facility Energy ÷ IT Equipment Energy
PUE of 1.0 is ideal (all energy used for IT)
Lower PUE indicates better energy efficiency
Industry average is around 1.5-2.0, best data centers achieve 1.1-1.4

question: Describe the 3 Rs of E-waste management and explain the importance of proper E-waste disposal.
expectedPoints: 3 Rs: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle
Reduce: Minimize waste generation through efficient product design and purchasing decisions
Reuse: Use products again for their original purpose or different purposes
Recycle: Process materials to create new products
Importance: Prevents toxic leaching, recovers valuable materials, reduces landfill burden, complies with regulations

question: Explain virtualization technology and how it contributes to energy efficiency in IT infrastructure.
expectedPoints: Virtualization allows multiple virtual machines to run on a single physical server
Reduces number of physical servers required
Improves server utilization from 15-30% to 60-80%
Reduces power and cooling requirements
Lower hardware costs and reduced e-waste
Examples: Server virtualization, desktop virtualization, storage virtualization

question: Discuss the role of cloud computing in achieving sustainability goals.
expectedPoints: Cloud computing enables shared infrastructure and resource pooling
Better utilization rates through multi-tenancy
Reduced need for on-premise hardware
Energy-efficient data centers operated by cloud providers
Scalability reduces resource wastage
Migration to cloud can significantly reduce carbon footprint
Enables use of renewable energy in large data centers

question: What is Sustainable Software Development? List four practices for developing energy-efficient software.
expectedPoints: Sustainable software development considers environmental impact throughout software lifecycle

Practices: 1) Algorithm efficiency - use optimized algorithms 2) Data storage optimization - compress data, delete unused data 3) Code optimization - minimize resource usage 4) Cloud-native development 5) Energy-aware programming 6) Reduce network transfers

question: Explain the concept of Carbon Footprint in the context of IT operations and methods to reduce it.
expectedPoints: Carbon footprint is total greenhouse gases (CO2 equivalent) produced by IT activities
Sources: Energy consumption for computing, data centers, manufacturing, disposal
Methods to reduce: 1) Use renewable energy 2) Virtualization 3) Cloud computing 4) Energy-efficient hardware 5) Power management 6) Remote work 7) Proper hardware disposal

question: Discuss in detail the strategies for implementing Green IT in an organization. Include technological, procedural, and policy-level strategies with specific examples.
expectedPoints: Technological Strategies: Virtualization, Cloud computing, Energy-efficient hardware, Power management, Thin clients, Solid-state drives, LED displays
Procedural Strategies: Data center consolidation, Server decommissioning, Desktop virtualization, Print reduction policies, Video conferencing
Policy-level Strategies: Green IT policies, E-waste management policy, Energy consumption standards, Vendor selection criteria based on environmental compliance, Employee training programs
Implementation Steps: Assessment → Planning → Implementation → Monitoring → Continuous improvement
Metrics to track: PUE, Energy consumption per user, Carbon emissions, E-waste diverted from landfills

question: Design a comprehensive Green IT policy for an organization. Include objectives, scope, key components, and implementation guidelines.
expectedPoints: Policy Objectives: Reduce environmental impact, Achieve regulatory compliance, Reduce costs, Improve corporate image
Scope: All IT operations, hardware procurement, software development, data center operations, employee practices

Key Components: 1) Energy efficiency standards 2) Hardware procurement guidelines (EPEAT, Energy Star) 3) E-waste management procedures 4) Virtualization requirements 5) Power management policies 6) Green travel and meetings policy 7) Reporting mechanisms
Implementation: Training programs, Regular audits, Metrics tracking, Management commitment, Third-party certifications
Review: Annual review and update of policy

question: Explain the environmental impact of data centers and discuss measures to make data centers more energy efficient and sustainable.
expectedPoints: Environmental Impact: High energy consumption, Carbon emissions, Water usage for cooling, Land use, E-waste generation
Energy Consumption: Data centers consume 1-2% of global electricity, growing at 10% annually

Measures for Energy Efficiency: 1) Improve PUE through efficient cooling 2) Free air cooling in cold climates 3) Hot/cold aisle containment 4) Renewable energy sources 5) Virtualization and consolidation 6) Energy-efficient servers 7) Power management 8) Location selection near renewable energy
Metrics: PUE, CUE (Carbon Usage Effectiveness), WUE (Water Usage Effectiveness), Renewable energy percentage

question: Discuss the concept of E-waste management in detail. Include sources of E-waste, hazards, management strategies, and relevant regulations.
expectedPoints: E-waste Sources: Obsolete computers, Mobile phones, Servers, Networking equipment, Peripherals, Consumer electronics
Hazards: Toxic materials (lead, mercury, cadmium, chromium), Air and water pollution, Health risks to workers
Management Strategies: 1) Prevention 2) Minimization 3) Reuse 4) Recycling 5) Proper disposal
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR): Manufacturers responsible for end-of-life management
Relevant Regulations: E-waste (Management) Rules 2016 in India, RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances), WEEE (Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment) Directive
Best Practices: Certified e-waste recyclers, Data destruction before disposal, Take-back programs, Design for environment
